questions.q: The INA trials were the court-martials of officers belonging to which force?
questions.explanation: The INA trials were the British court-martials of officers of the Indian National Army after the Second World War.

questions.q: At which famous Delhi landmark were the INA trials held?
questions.explanation: The trials were held at the Red Fort in Delhi, giving them the name 'Red Fort Trials.'

questions.q: In which years were the main INA trials conducted?
questions.explanation: The INA trials took place in 1945 and 1946, after Japan's defeat in the Second World War.

questions.q: By what alternative name are the INA trials commonly known?
questions.explanation: Because they were held at the Red Fort, the INA trials are also called the Red Fort Trials.

questions.q: What was the public reaction across India to the INA trials?
options: Widespread support for the British prosecution
Huge sympathy and nationwide protests in support of the accused
questions.explanation: The trials provoked enormous public sympathy and protests across India in support of the accused officers.

questions.q: The accused INA officers were charged primarily with what kind of offence under British law?
options: Treason or waging war against the King
questions.explanation: The officers were charged chiefly with treason, or waging war against the King-Emperor.

questions.q: Which political party took a leading role in organizing the legal defence of the INA prisoners?
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress organized a prominent defence committee for the accused INA officers.

questions.q: The INA whose officers were tried had been led during the war by which figure?
questions.explanation: The INA had been led by Subhas Chandra Bose, whose memory loomed large over the trials.

questions.q: What effect did the INA trials have on the demand for independence?
questions.explanation: The trials galvanized nationalist feeling and added momentum to the demand for independence.

questions.q: The INA trials are often seen as a turning point because they affected the loyalty of which institution?
options: The Indian armed forces under the British
questions.explanation: The trials shook British confidence in the loyalty of Indian soldiers in the colonial armed forces.

questions.q: The first and most famous INA trial featured three officers from which different religious communities, symbolizing unity?
options: A Hindu, a Muslim and a Sikh
questions.explanation: The first trial of Shah Nawaz Khan, Prem Sahgal and Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon represented Muslim, Hindu and Sikh communities.

questions.q: Which three officers were the accused in the celebrated first INA trial of 1945?
options: Shah Nawaz Khan, Prem Sahgal and Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon
questions.explanation: The first joint trial was of Shah Nawaz Khan, Prem Kumar Sahgal and Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon.

questions.q: Which prominent Congress leader and future Prime Minister joined the defence of the INA accused, donning his legal robes again?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru, a barrister, joined the INA Defence Committee and appeared for the accused.

questions.q: Which famous lawyer and later Congress leader led the INA defence team?
questions.explanation: Bhulabhai Desai led the defence team for the INA accused in the first trial.

questions.q: What was the broad outcome for the three officers found guilty in the first INA trial?
options: They were exiled to the Andamans
Their sentences were remitted and they were released
questions.explanation: Although convicted, their transportation sentences were remitted by the Commander-in-Chief amid public pressure, and they were freed.

questions.q: Which slogan associated with the INA defence became a rallying cry during the trials?
options: Lal Qila se aayi awaz / release of INA prisoners
questions.explanation: Slogans demanding the release of the INA prisoners, raised around the Red Fort, became a national rallying cry.

questions.q: The INA trials contributed to unrest that helped trigger which 1946 revolt among the armed forces?
questions.explanation: Anger over the trials fed into the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny of February 1946.

questions.q: Public agitation over the INA trials was especially intense in which city, a hub of protest in late 1945?
questions.explanation: Calcutta saw especially fierce protests and clashes in November 1945 over the INA trials.

questions.q: The trials presented the British with a dilemma because punishing the officers risked what?
options: Provoking mass unrest and disaffection in the army
questions.explanation: Harsh punishment risked igniting mass unrest and undermining the loyalty of Indian troops, so the British acted cautiously.

questions.q: Besides the Congress, even some otherwise rival groups supported the INA accused. This reflected what about the trials?
options: They were of interest only to soldiers
They had broad cross-party national appeal
They were a purely religious matter
questions.explanation: Support for the accused cut across political divisions, showing the trials' broad national appeal.

questions.q: The first INA trial at the Red Fort opened in which month of 1945?
questions.explanation: The first joint court-martial of the three officers began in November 1945 at the Red Fort.

questions.q: Which officer in the famous first trial had earlier been a captain in the British Indian Army's 1st Punjab Regiment before joining the INA?
questions.explanation: Shah Nawaz Khan, a former British Indian Army officer, was among the three tried in the first case.

questions.q: Apart from Bhulabhai Desai and Nehru, which eminent jurist also assisted the INA Defence Committee?
questions.explanation: Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru was among the eminent lawyers who assisted the INA Defence Committee.

questions.q: A key defence argument in the INA trials held that the accused were what, rather than traitors?
options: Soldiers of a recognized provisional government
questions.explanation: The defence argued the accused were lawful combatants of the Azad Hind provisional government, not traitors to be tried for treason.

questions.q: Who was the British Commander-in-Chief in India who ultimately remitted the sentences of the first trial's convicted officers?
questions.explanation: Commander-in-Chief Claude Auchinleck remitted the transportation sentences, freeing the officers amid the public outcry.

questions.q: The protests over the INA trials in Calcutta in November 1945 are notable for involving cooperation between which groups?
options: Students, Congress, League and Communist supporters
questions.explanation: The Calcutta demonstrations drew together students and supporters of the Congress, the League and the Communists in a rare show of unity.

questions.q: Why did the British decide, after the first cases, to limit further INA prosecutions to those accused of brutality rather than mere treason?
options: The trials were inflaming nationalist opinion dangerously
questions.explanation: Faced with surging nationalist anger, the authorities narrowed later prosecutions to charges of brutality or murder rather than waging war.

questions.q: The INA trials are often said to have shown the British that they could no longer rely on what to hold India?
options: The loyalty of the Indian Army
questions.explanation: The trials and the sympathy they generated convinced many that Britain could no longer count on the Indian Army's loyalty.

questions.q: Which officer tried in the first INA case later became a Congress politician and minister in independent India?
questions.explanation: Shah Nawaz Khan went on to join the Congress and serve as a union minister in independent India.

questions.q: Historians often argue the INA trials accelerated independence chiefly through which mechanism?
options: Eroding British confidence in the armed forces' reliability
questions.explanation: By eroding British confidence in the loyalty of Indian armed forces, the trials are seen as hastening the decision to quit India.
